feed_card.headline: The Mediterranean Sea dried up almost completely, then the Atlantic refilled it in under two years.
teasers: The salt a 1970 drill ship struck
A flood that ran a thousand Amazons
What sealed the door to the Atlantic

sections.content: The Mediterranean Sea dried up almost completely, then the Atlantic refilled it in under two years.

sections.content: A sea looks permanent. Seal one off and the intuition says it just sits there. The Mediterranean, once a shift in the crust closed the strait at Gibraltar, evaporated faster than the rivers feeding it could keep up. Over roughly 600,000 years it drew down kilometers, until the deep basins may have lain 3 to 5 km below the world ocean, their floors crusted white with salt. Then the barrier failed. The Atlantic poured back in and filled most of the basin within about two years.

items: It took the sea roughly 600,000 years to dry out, and under two years for the Atlantic to put most of the water back.
At the peak, the gap at Gibraltar carried about a thousand times the flow of the Amazon.
Inside the basin the water rose more than 10 meters a day, the height of a three-storey building.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='56' y1='56' x2='56' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='56' y1='250' x2='362' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='56' y1='68' x2='350' y2='68' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.3' stroke-dasharray='6 6' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='62' y='62' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>full</text><path d='M56 250 C 92 176 120 116 168 102 C 232 86 300 82 344 82' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='344' cy='82' r='4' fill='var(--accent)'/><text x='340' y='120' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>90% refilled</text><text x='210' y='276' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>months to 2 years</text><text x='32' y='152' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 32 152)'>basin filled</text></svg>

sections.content: The proof of the speed is carved in rock. Drilling and seismic surveys under the strait found a channel gouged clean across the Gibraltar sill, about 200 km long and cut more than 250 m into bedrock. A slow, rising tide does not do that. Only a torrent does. In 2009 a team led by Daniel Garcia-Castellanos fed the channel's shape into an erosion model and worked backward from the damage. A little water spilling over the sill cut a groove, the groove funnelled more water, and the faster flow cut deeper still. The water ended up running faster than 40 meters a second, on the order of 100 million cubic meters every second. That is where the two-year figure comes from.

content.body: On 23 August 1970, the drill ship Glomar Challenger pulled a core from deep under the Mediterranean seabed. It came up full of gypsum and rock salt, the minerals seawater leaves when it dries. The two lead scientists, William Ryan and Kenneth Hsü, read it and drew an absurd-sounding conclusion. This whole sea had once been a desert. Salt like that forms where shallow water bakes in the sun, and a deep seabed is the last place it belongs. The idea was mocked for years. Then the cores, the salt beds, and the drowned river canyons settled it. Hsü told the story later in a book he called *The Mediterranean Was a Desert*.

key_figures.name: Kenneth Hsü
key_figures.one_line: Co-chief scientist on the voyage that cored the salt and argued the whole Mediterranean had once been a desert.

key_figures.name: William B. F. Ryan
key_figures.one_line: Marine geologist who co-directed Leg 13 and helped read the deep-sea salt cores as the floor of a vanished sea.

content.title: Rivers gouged canyons deeper than the Grand Canyon
content.body: As the sea fell, the rivers feeding it had to chase it down. The Nile carved a gorge into its own bed, reaching 2,500 m below today's sea level near Cairo. That is deeper than the Grand Canyon. It now lies buried deep beneath the delta. The Rhône, the Po, and the Ebro left the same drowned canyons under their deltas. Those hidden gorges are among the clearest traces the drying left behind.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='24' y1='70' x2='376' y2='70' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='24' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='start'>Nile delta plain</text><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.22' stroke-linecap='round'><line x1='24' y1='112' x2='150' y2='112'/><line x1='250' y1='112' x2='376' y2='112'/><line x1='24' y1='152' x2='128' y2='152'/><line x1='272' y1='152' x2='376' y2='152'/></g><path d='M150 70 L200 238 L250 70 Z' fill='var(--accent)' opacity='0.08'/><path d='M150 70 L200 238 L250 70' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='200' y='126' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>buried gorge</text><line x1='306' y1='76' x2='306' y2='232' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='314' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='start'>2,500 m down</text></svg>

content.title: The crisis nearly wiped the sea's life clean
content.body: A dead, hypersaline basin is no place to live. Most of what swam in the Mediterranean did not survive the crisis. A 2024 study tracked species across the event and found turnover above 30% in every group, from plankton to whales. Of the sea's endemic species, the ones found nowhere else, only 86 came through. When the Atlantic broke back in, it carried a new cast with it. Much of today's Mediterranean life traces back to those Atlantic newcomers. The old residents had died in the brine.

sections.content: In the length of one warm-and-cold swing of the climate, a sea the size of a continent emptied to a salt pan and then came roaring back. The coastline every later empire fought over is younger than the flood that filled it. Nothing on the map was inevitable. The rock beneath the water keeps its own deep time, and once in a great while it moves fast enough to watch.

content.question: Why did the Mediterranean dry out during the crisis?
options: Global sea level fell so far that the whole basin was left high and dry
The regional climate turned to desert and rainfall stopped entirely
The Gibraltar gateway closed, and evaporation removed water faster than rivers replaced it
Undersea volcanism heated the basin and boiled it away
content.explanation: With the Atlantic sealed off, the only large input was river inflow. In a hot, dry basin that could not balance evaporation, so the sea shrank and salt piled up on the floor.

content.question: The refill happened far faster than the drying. What made it so abrupt?
options: An earthquake instantly shattered the land bridge at Gibraltar
Water overtopping the barrier cut a channel that let ever more water through
Heavy rainfall refilled the basin from the sky within months
The basin floor sank, pulling Atlantic water in
content.explanation: Once a little water spilled over the sill, it opened a channel that let ever more flow through, deepening as it went. That runaway feedback turned a leak into a flood moving on the order of 100 million cubic meters a second.

content.question: What first convinced scientists that the basin had dried out, rather than staying full?
options: Deep cores brought up salt and gypsum, minerals that form where seawater evaporates
Fossils of desert plants were found scattered across the seabed
The sea today is saltier than any other on Earth
Ancient maps showed the basin as dry land
content.explanation: Rock salt and gypsum precipitate when seawater dries. Finding thick layers of them on the deep floor meant that floor had once been an evaporating surface, not two kilometers underwater.

content.question: How did the peak flow of the Zanclean flood compare to rivers today?
options: Roughly equal to the Amazon
About ten times the Amazon
Less than the Nile in flood
Around a thousand times the discharge of the Amazon
content.explanation: The peak discharge, on the order of 100 million cubic meters a second, was about three orders of magnitude beyond the Amazon, the largest river flowing today.

content.question: What happened to Mediterranean sea life across the crisis?
options: It was unaffected, because species sheltered in deep brine pools
The same species returned once the water came back
Most species were lost, and the sea was largely repopulated from the Atlantic
The basin stayed lifeless for millions of years afterward
content.explanation: Turnover topped 30% in every group, and only 86 of the sea's endemic species survived. When Gibraltar reopened, Atlantic species poured in and became much of the modern Mediterranean's life.

content.question: What broader point does the episode make about Earth's surface?
options: Familiar geography can change catastrophically fast, not only over slow ages
Sea levels have held perfectly steady for millions of years
Evaporation is far too weak to reshape an ocean basin
Continents never move once they are in place
content.explanation: The basin emptied over ages but refilled in months. Its coastline is younger than the flood that made it, a sign the map can shift within a span short enough to witness.

content.myth: It all flooded back in a matter of months.
content.reality: That is the fast phase, which moved roughly 90% of the water in a few months to two years. A slower spillover may have leaked in for far longer before the barrier broke.

content.myth: Once it dried, the basin was clean desert from shore to shore.
content.reality: The lowest ground more likely held dense brine lakes and salt flats than open sand, where some hardy life clung on.

content.myth: The drop and the flood were a single sudden event.
content.reality: In truth the drawdown and the salt built up in pulses across some 600,000 years. Only the final refill came fast.

content.body: The pattern holds. Its scale is what is still argued. Just how far the sea's surface fell, and how completely the basin dried, are read differently from the same sediments. Some recent work on the buried Nile canyon points to a smaller drop than the classic kilometers-deep picture.
items: How far did the sea draw down, hundreds of meters or whole kilometers?
Did it dry and refill in cycles, or fall once and stay low until the flood?
How much water seeped in slowly before the barrier gave way for good?

content.label: Garcia-Castellanos D, et al. Catastrophic flood of the Mediterranean after the Messinian salinity crisis. Nature 2009;462:778-781
Krijgsman W, et al. Chronology, causes and progression of the Messinian salinity crisis. Nature 1999;400:652-655
Agiadi K, et al. The marine biodiversity impact of the Late Miocene Mediterranean salinity crisis. Science 2024;385:986-991
Causes and consequences of the Messinian salinity crisis. Nature Reviews Earth & Environment 2024
